title: Listening - Part 4: Recording Local History
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Dan, who makes a podcast about local history. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Dan Morris, creator of a podcast about the history of his coastal town. Dan, did you study history at university? || Dan: No, I trained as an accountant, although I always loved listening to people’s stories. The podcast began quite personally. [24]When my grandmother moved house, I recorded her memories of working in a seaside hotel because I didn’t want those stories to disappear[/24]. Friends who heard the recording said other local people would have memories worth saving too. || Presenter: Was producing the first episodes straightforward? || Dan: Technically it was simpler than I’d feared. You can learn recording basics fairly quickly. The difficult part came afterwards. [25]I had hours of wonderful material, and deciding what to leave out was much harder than finding enough to include[/25]. Listeners need a clear story; they can’t hear every interesting detail. || Presenter: Where do you interview people? || Dan: I used to invite everyone into a quiet room because the sound quality was easier to control. Now, whenever possible, we meet in the place they’re describing. [26]Standing outside an old cinema or on the harbour wall often brings back memories that would never appear in an indoor interview[/26]. Of course, I still have to edit out wind and traffic. || Presenter: How have local people responded? || Dan: I imagined most listeners would be school students using the podcast for projects. Some are, but the strongest reaction has come from older residents. [27]They contact me with corrections, photographs and extra stories after hearing an episode[/27]. At first I worried about getting something wrong; now I see those messages as part of creating a fuller record. || Presenter: Have you had any professional training? || Dan: I attended a short radio course last winter. I didn’t need help asking questions, but [28]I learned how to edit pauses and background sound so an episode moves naturally without feeling rushed[/28]. That has made a big difference. Earlier episodes seem rather slow to me now. || Presenter: Is there anything you wish you had done differently at the start? || Dan: Yes. I was nervous, so I spoke too much myself. [29]In my first few episodes, I explained information that my interviewees could have expressed more vividly in their own words[/29]. These days I’m happier to let people tell their story, even if the conversation takes an unexpected direction. || Presenter: What’s next for the podcast? || Dan: I don’t want it to become only a collection of memories from the distant past. [30]My next series will ask teenagers how they experience the town today, so future listeners can compare different generations[/30]. Local history is being created now, not only remembered. || Presenter: Dan, thank you. || Dan: It’s been a pleasure.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What led Dan to start recording local history?

options.A: He was asked to produce material for schools.
options.B: He wanted to preserve a relative's memories.
options.C: He found old recordings in a family home.

questions.explanation: Dan began by recording his grandmother's memories before they were lost.

questions.question: What did Dan find most difficult in the beginning?

options.A: Choosing which recorded material to use.
options.B: Persuading local people to be interviewed.
options.C: Learning how to operate recording equipment.

questions.explanation: He found selecting material to leave out harder than collecting enough content.

questions.question: Why does Dan now interview people in outdoor locations?

options.A: The locations help people remember more.
options.B: There is less unwanted background noise.
options.C: Listeners enjoy hearing local traffic and weather.

questions.explanation: He says being in the place described can bring back memories that indoor interviews do not.

questions.question: How have older residents reacted to the podcast?

options.A: They have requested simpler historical explanations.
options.B: They have offered additional information about episodes.
options.C: They have complained about the focus on younger audiences.

questions.explanation: Older residents send corrections, photos and further stories after listening.

questions.question: What benefit did Dan get from his radio course?

options.A: He became more confident at interviewing strangers.
options.B: He learned to select more interesting subjects.
options.C: He improved the pace and sound of his episodes.

questions.explanation: The course taught him how to edit pauses and background sounds so episodes flow better.

questions.question: What does Dan regret about his early episodes?

options.A: He included too much of his own narration.
options.B: He allowed interviews to go off topic.
options.C: He published them before checking facts.

questions.explanation: He says he explained things himself that interviewees could have said more vividly.

questions.question: What does Dan plan to do in his next series?

options.A: Compare his town with other coastal areas.
options.B: Record the experiences of younger local people.
options.C: Focus on changes to the tourist industry.

questions.explanation: His next series will invite teenagers to describe the town as they know it now.
